summary: The paper introduces Multi², a hierarchical framework for LLM-based agents operating in long-horizon, text-based interactive environments. The central idea is to decompose an agent into two role-specialized modules that share a frozen backbone but use separate LoRA adapters: a high-level planner (System 1), trained with supervised fine-tuning to emit context-aware sub-goals, and a low-level executor (System 2), trained with an offline-to-online reinforcement learning pipeline to select atomic actions. The offline stage adopts an IQL-style value objective together with an advantage-weighted actor loss, and the online stage adds a KL penalty anchoring the policy to its offline counterpart to stabilize self-improvement. The stated aim is to combat objective drift over extended interactions and to improve token efficiency by invoking the planner only on demand. The authors evaluate on ScienceWorld, ALFWorld, and TextCraft with three backbones spanning different scales and families, reporting consistent gains over prompt-based baselines (ReAct, Reflexion, ADaPT) and training-based ones (GRPO and the hierarchical method Glider), along with reduced token usage and a widening advantage as task difficulty grows. The paper also contributes three hierarchical benchmark datasets constructed via a deterministic extraction pipeline.
strengths_and_weaknesses:
The problem is well chosen and the framework is soundly motivated. Separating semantic planning from environment interaction and matching each to an appropriate training signal—SFT for the planner and RL for the executor—is a natural and defensible design, and the paper backs it up with a targeted ablation: swapping the roles (RL for the planner, SFT for the executor) produces the worst configuration, pure RL for high-level planning is unstable with high variance, and an SFT-only executor is brittle under environmental variation. The empirical coverage is a genuine strength: three environments with complementary interaction patterns and reward structures, evaluated across three backbones of different scales and families, which lends credibility to the generality of the approach. The token-efficiency analysis is a practical and welcome addition—invoking System 1 only upon sub-goal completion and letting RL shape well-formed actions yields better performance at markedly lower inference cost. The most compelling result is the behavior under longer horizons and harder tasks, where the margin over baselines grows rather than collapses; this directly speaks to the objective-drift problem the paper sets out to address. Finally, the release of the three hierarchical datasets, extraction rules, and training code—built through a deterministic, code-based pipeline rather than LLM-dependent extraction—is a solid reproducibility and community contribution, and the use of an anonymous project page rather than a post-review promise is appreciated. The presentation is clear and the methodology is broadly easy to follow.

My main reservations concern attribution and originality. Multi² changes several things at once—separate adapters, role-specialized SFT+RL training, a modified RL loss, and an offline-to-online schedule—so it is difficult to know which factor drives the gains. The ablations help but do not isolate the contribution of the hierarchy itself; a single-agent baseline trained with the same offline-to-online objective would clarify how much comes from hierarchical decomposition versus the RL pipeline. More pointedly, a claimed distinction from Glider is the use of separate role-specific adapters rather than a shared adapter with prompt-based role separation, yet there is no controlled ablation that holds the pipeline fixed and swaps separate adapters for a shared one—for a central claim this omission is significant. Relatedly, most ablations are confined to ScienceWorld, which is thin support for broad claims about long-horizon reliability across environments. The empirical claims would also be more convincing with statistical robustness reporting: the main tables give point estimates only, and given the stochasticity of these environments, multi-seed means and standard errors are needed. The online stage in particular is under-characterized in terms of interaction cost, wall-clock training time, and stability across seeds. On the algorithmic side, there is an unresolved tension in the offline actor loss: the value learning follows IQL, whose core principle is never to query Q on out-of-distribution actions, but the policy-anchored advantage term evaluates the advantage on actions sampled from the current policy, which can drift off-distribution and reintroduce the overestimation IQL is designed to avoid; the paper offers no Q-value diagnostics to rule this out. The sub-goal completion mechanism is also under-specified—how completion is detected and how the system tolerates mis-detections is important to the robustness of the planner–executor interface—and because System 1 is frozen online, the framework has no way to correct a poor sub-goal decomposition or to replan when the executor fails, which seems especially relevant out of distribution.

On significance and originality, the individual building blocks—hierarchical decomposition, SFT-based planning, IQL-style offline RL, and KL-regularized online adaptation—are all well-established, so the contribution lies in their integration rather than in any new learning algorithm. The "multi-agent" framing is arguably a stretch, since the two modules share a single backbone and differ only through role-specific adapters and prompts; "hierarchical" is the more accurate description. That said, the integration is principled and the practical payoff—robustness at long horizons together with the released datasets—is real, which is where I see the paper's value.
key_questions_for_authors:
1. A central claimed advantage over Glider is the use of separate role-specific LoRA adapters rather than a shared adapter with prompt-based role separation. Can you provide a controlled ablation that holds the training pipeline fixed and replaces the two separate adapters with a single shared one, to isolate the contribution of parameter decoupling?
2. How much of the gain comes from the hierarchical decomposition itself versus the offline-to-online RL pipeline? A single-agent baseline trained with the same RL objective would help, as would extending the ablations beyond ScienceWorld to all three environments.
3. Could you report multi-seed statistics (mean and standard error) for the main results, along with the online RL stage's interaction cost, training time, and stability curves across seeds?
4. The policy-anchored advantage term in the offline actor loss evaluates Q on current-policy actions, which can be out-of-distribution and contradicts IQL's design of never querying Q off-distribution. Does Q-value inflation occur during training, and have you considered evaluating the advantage only on dataset actions or using a conservative Q estimate for this term?
5. How is sub-goal completion detected during execution, and how sensitive is performance to errors in this signal? Relatedly, does System 1 have any ability to replan when System 2 encounters failure modes, particularly out of distribution?
limitations: A dedicated limitations discussion is largely missing and would strengthen the paper. Two trade-offs in particular deserve explicit treatment: freezing System 1 throughout online training leaves the planner wholly dependent on the quality of the offline sub-goal decompositions with no mechanism for self-correction, and the offline actor loss's policy-anchored advantage term sits in tension with IQL's avoidance of out-of-distribution Q queries.
